title: Drop scones (Scotch pancakes)
subtitle: Small, thick griddle pancakes from the Scottish baking tradition
excerpt: The Scottish drop scone: a thick, lightly sweetened batter dropped in spoonfuls onto a hot griddle and cooked until small bubbles appear on the surface, then flipped to finish. Soft, spongy, served warm with butter.
sourceNotes: Drop scones, also called Scotch pancakes, are a fixture of Scottish home baking, documented in Scottish cookery literature from the eighteenth century. F. Marian McNeill covers them in The Scots Kitchen (1929), noting their widespread use across Scotland as a griddle bread. They are distinct from American pancakes (thinner, larger) and English crumpets (yeast-raised, with holes). The name 'drop scone' refers to the method of dropping spoonfuls of batter onto the griddle. This recipe follows the classic Scottish approach.

content.text: Drop scones are cooked entirely on a
and require no oven. The batter is straightforward but the heat of the pan must be right. The
is the definitive guide to when to flip: when small bubbles dot the surface and the edges look dry, the scone is ready to turn.

attrs.prepNote: melted, plus extra for the pan

content.text: Sift the flour, sugar, baking powder, and salt into a bowl. Make a well in the centre. Add the eggs and half the milk and whisk from the centre outward until a smooth, thick batter forms. Add the remaining milk and melted butter and whisk until just combined. Rest for 5 minutes.

Heat a heavy frying pan or griddle over medium heat. Rub with a little butter on a piece of kitchen paper. Drop heaped tablespoons of batter onto the surface, leaving space between them. They spread slightly. Cook until small bubbles appear across the surface and the edges look set and dry, about 2 minutes. Flip with a palette knife or spatula and cook for a further 1 to 2 minutes until the underside is golden. Keep warm in a folded tea towel while you cook the remainder. Wipe the pan between batches and rebutter lightly.
